name: Cedar Mountain area
short_description: Free BLM rockhounding in central Utah's scenic high desert. Hunt for colorful agate and jasper specimens on Cedar Mountain with stunning views of the Henry Mountains and San Rafael Swell.
description:
Cedar Mountain area offers accessible rockhounding on public BLM land in Emery County, Utah. This high-elevation site sits between 7,500 and 8,500 feet, providing a cooler collecting experience compared to Utah's lower desert regions. The area is known for producing quality specimens of banded agate, colorful jasper, and silica-rich chalcedony found scattered across hillsides and in weathered washes. The surrounding landscape features dramatic geological formations with views of the Henry Mountains to the south and the San Rafael Swell to the north, making this a scenic destination for both collectors and photographers.

Geologically, Cedar Mountain sits within the Colorado Plateau where Cretaceous sedimentary rocks have been cut and uplifted, exposing layers rich in silica deposits. The agate and jasper specimens here formed from ancient hydrothermal fluids that percolated through fractures and cavities in sandstone and shale over millions of years. Erosion and weathering have exposed these gemstones at the surface, making them accessible to surface collectors without digging.

Rockhounders should expect to walk hillsides with scattered sage and pinyon-juniper vegetation, examining surface material for colorful specimens. Most productive areas are found on gentle to moderate slopes where erosion has concentrated heavier specimens. The high elevation means cooler temperatures year-round but also brings afternoon thunderstorms during monsoon season. Parking areas provide access to multiple ridges and washes within easy walking distance.

This location is ideal for beginners and families seeking free, permit-free rockhounding in a scenic setting. The combination of productive terrain, reasonable accessibility, and lack of fees makes Cedar Mountain a popular choice for Utah rockhounders. Respect for the landscape and adherence to the 25-pound daily collection limit ensures this site remains open for future collectors.
history: Cedar Mountain sits within the Colorado Plateau province where Cretaceous marine and continental deposits accumulated over 100 million years ago. During the Laramide orogeny, tectonic uplift and folding created the structural highs and lows visible today, while later hydrothermal fluids deposited silica minerals in fractures and void spaces, creating the agate and jasper specimens now exposed by natural erosion.
tips: Search hillsides with light-colored soil where darker agate and jasper specimens contrast against the background - concentrate on benches and wash banks where erosion has concentrated heavier stones. Look for banded or striped patterns in red, orange, brown, and cream colors typical of Utah's High Plateau agates. Bring a hand lens to examine color banding and translucency - high-quality specimens show distinct patterns and polish well. Early morning or late afternoon visits provide better lighting for spotting specimens, and avoid the area during afternoon thunderstorm season.
terrain_notes: Elevation ranges from 7,500 to 8,500 feet with rolling hills covered in sagebrush and scattered pinyon-juniper woodland. Terrain consists of gentle to moderate slopes with scattered exposed bedrock and shale outcrops. Walking surfaces are rocky with some loose stones; sturdy hiking boots are strongly recommended. Most collecting areas are within 0.5 to 2 miles of parking pullouts, with relatively easy walking on established game trails and washes.
written_directions: From Castle Dale, take State Route 29 south toward Huntington for approximately 15 miles. Continue south on US 191 for about 8 miles. Turn east onto County Road 435 (Buckhorn Wash Road) and drive approximately 10 miles on a rough but passable dirt road. Look for informal BLM parking pullouts on either side of the road. Parking areas provide direct access to surrounding hillsides and washes where agate and jasper specimens can be found.
road_conditions: Rough dirt road with washboard texture and occasional rocks. High-clearance vehicles recommended but standard passenger vehicles can navigate slowly. Road may be muddy or impassable during heavy rain or snowmelt.
parking_notes: Informal BLM pullouts on either side of County Road 435. Multiple parking areas available with capacity for 3-6 vehicles each. No facilities or developed parking lot.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per Federal Land Policy and Management Act (FLPMA). No commercial collection. No motorized excavation or power tools. Surface collection only - no digging or destroying geological formations. Remove all trash and leave no trace.
rules:

No motorized excavation or power tools

Pack out all trash
nearest_services: Castle Dale is approximately 25 miles north and offers gas, food, water, and lodging. Huntington is approximately 20 miles west with similar services.
accessibility_notes: Relatively easy terrain suitable for most hikers and families. Gentle to moderate slopes with stable walking surfaces. Unpaved dirt road to parking areas but parking pullouts provide direct access without additional hiking. Sturdy footwear recommended for rocky ground. No wheelchair accessibility on the terrain itself, but vehicle access to parking areas is straightforward.

faq.question: What gemstones can you find rockhounding at Cedar Mountain in Utah?
faq.answer: Cedar Mountain is known for producing quality agate, jasper, chalcedony, and chert specimens. The most common finds are banded agates in red, orange, brown, and cream colors, along with colorful jasper nodules. Most specimens are small to medium-sized pieces suitable for cabochon cutting or display collections.

faq.question: Do I need a permit to rockhound at Cedar Mountain area near Emery County?
faq.answer: No permit is required for recreational rockhounding at Cedar Mountain. The site sits on BLM public land where free, permit-free surface collection is allowed under the Federal Land Policy and Management Act. You only need to follow the 25-pound daily limit and collect for personal use only.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to rockhound Cedar Mountain, Utah?
faq.answer: The best rockhounding season at Cedar Mountain is June through September when temperatures are moderate and snow is absent. The site sits at 7,500-8,500 feet elevation where summers are cool compared to lower Utah deserts. Avoid July and August afternoons when monsoonal thunderstorms develop rapidly. Spring and fall offer excellent conditions but may have lingering snow in early June or late September.

faq.question: How far is Cedar Mountain from the nearest town and what are the driving conditions?
faq.answer: Cedar Mountain is approximately 25 miles south of Castle Dale. Access requires driving about 18 miles on US 191 and County Road 435, a rough but passable dirt road that is best driven with a high-clearance vehicle. Standard passenger cars can navigate the route slowly in dry conditions, but heavy rain may make the road temporarily impassable. Allow 45 minutes to 1 hour from Castle Dale.

faq.question: Is Cedar Mountain good for beginner rockhounders and families with kids?
faq.answer: Yes, Cedar Mountain is excellent for beginners and families. The terrain is relatively easy with gentle slopes and good walking surfaces. Specimens are accessible on the surface without digging equipment, making it suitable for children ages 6 and older. The high elevation keeps temperatures cool, and the scenic setting of the Henry Mountains and San Rafael Swell provides educational value alongside rockhounding.

faq.question: What tools and supplies should I bring for rockhounding at Cedar Mountain?
faq.answer: Bring sturdy hiking boots for rocky terrain, a hand lens or magnifier to examine specimens, collection bags to carry your finds (limited to 25 lbs), sunscreen, hat, and plenty of water. A small chisel or rock hammer can help examine specimens but no power tools or motorized equipment are allowed. Bring a map or GPS device as cell service is spotty. Pack out all trash and consider bringing a backpack to distribute weight evenly.

meta_title: Cedar Mountain Rockhounding - Find Agate in Utah
meta_description: Free BLM rockhounding for agate and jasper at Cedar Mountain, Utah. No permit required. Hunt scenic high desert hills near Emery County.
